title_en: The Quranic Declaration and Biblical Evidence Together
title_id: Deklarasi Al-Quran dan Bukti Alkitab Bersama
content_en:
The culminating argument of Deedat's analysis brings together the Quranic declaration of Muhammad's prophethood with the biblical evidence he has surveyed across the preceding chapters, demonstrating their mutual coherence and their convergence upon a single conclusion: the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) was foretold in the earlier scriptures and his coming represented the fulfillment of the Abrahamic prophetic tradition. The Quran explicitly states: 'Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel' (7:157) — a verse that presupposes the existence of prophetic references to Muhammad within the earlier scriptures.

This Quranic claim — that the coming of Muhammad was recorded in the Torah and the Gospel — has profound implications for the Islamic understanding of Quranic reliability. If the Quran declares, as an empirically verifiable claim, that the earlier scriptures contain prophecies about the Prophet, and if those scriptures do indeed contain such passages upon careful examination, this convergence constitutes a powerful argument for the divine origin of the Quran. The unlettered man from Makkah who could not read the Hebrew Torah or the Greek Gospels claimed that they contained prophecies about him — and the textual evidence Deedat has assembled suggests that this claim is well-founded.

The Prophet Muhammad himself is recorded in the hadith literature as having said: 'I am the supplication of Ibrahim, the glad tiding of Isa, and the dream of my mother.' The 'supplication of Ibrahim' refers to Ibrahim's prayer recorded in the Quran (2:129): 'Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them.' The 'glad tiding of Isa' refers to the Quranic verse (61:6) in which Jesus announces the coming of Ahmad. These two explicit Quranic passages — Ibrahim's supplication and Jesus's announcement — are matched by the broader pattern of Old Testament prophecy that Deedat's analysis has uncovered.

The unlettered nature of the Prophet Muhammad is itself a significant theological datum in this context. A man who could not read or write could not have searched the Hebrew and Greek texts of the earlier scriptures for prophetic passages to claim as his own. His knowledge of the earlier scriptures — both the accurate information they contained and the alterations they had undergone — came, the Islamic tradition affirms, through divine revelation. This explains how the Quran can accurately identify the content of the earlier scriptures without the Prophet having access to them through normal scholarly means.

Deedat concludes by noting that the cumulative weight of the biblical evidence he has presented — Deuteronomy 18:18 with its description of a prophet 'like Moses' from among the brothers of Israel, the Paraclete passages in John's Gospel with their description of a human messenger who would speak what he heard and glorify Jesus, and the Song of Solomon's reference to the machmaddim — is not easily dismissed as coincidence or wishful interpretation. Each piece of evidence has its own force, and together they constitute a coherent case for the biblical attestation of Muhammad's prophethood.

For Muslims reading this work, the significance is one of confirmation and deepened appreciation: the Prophet they follow was not an unexpected historical accident but the fulfillment of a divine plan announced across the centuries through a chain of prophets. For Christians engaging honestly with the evidence Deedat presents, the significance is one of invitation: to approach the Prophet of Islam not as a stranger to their own tradition but as one foretold within it, and to consider seriously the claim that the Quran he brought represents the final and preserved form of the divine word that God has sent to all humanity.
content_id:
Argumen puncak analisis Deedat menyatukan deklarasi Al-Quran tentang kenabian Muhammad ﷺ dengan bukti alkitabiah yang telah ia kaji di seluruh bab-bab sebelumnya, mendemonstrasikan koherensi timbal balik mereka dan konvergensi mereka menuju satu kesimpulan: Nabi Muhammad ﷺ telah dinubuatkan dalam kitab-kitab suci terdahulu dan kedatangannya merupakan pemenuhan tradisi kenabian Abrahami. Al-Quran secara eksplisit menyatakan: 'Yaitu orang-orang yang mengikut Rasul, Nabi yang ummi yang mereka dapati tertulis di dalam Taurat dan Injil yang ada di sisi mereka' (7:157) — sebuah ayat yang mengandaikan keberadaan referensi-referensi kenabian kepada Muhammad ﷺ dalam kitab-kitab suci terdahulu.

Klaim Al-Quran ini — bahwa kedatangan Muhammad ﷺ dicatat dalam Taurat dan Injil — memiliki implikasi mendalam bagi pemahaman Islam tentang keandalan Al-Quran. Jika Al-Quran menyatakan, sebagai klaim yang dapat diverifikasi secara empiris, bahwa kitab-kitab suci terdahulu mengandung nubuatan-nubuatan tentang Nabi ﷺ, dan jika kitab-kitab suci tersebut memang mengandung bagian-bagian semacam itu setelah pemeriksaan yang cermat, konvergensi ini merupakan argumen yang kuat untuk asal ilahi Al-Quran. Orang yang ummi dari Makkah yang tidak dapat membaca Taurat Ibrani atau Injil Yunani mengklaim bahwa mereka mengandung nubuatan-nubuatan tentangnya — dan bukti tekstual yang telah dikumpulkan Deedat menunjukkan bahwa klaim ini beralasan.

Nabi Muhammad ﷺ sendiri dicatat dalam literatur hadis sebagai pernah berkata: 'Aku adalah doa Ibrahim, kabar gembira Isa, dan impian ibuku.' 'Doa Ibrahim' merujuk kepada doa Ibrahim yang dicatat dalam Al-Quran (2:129): 'Ya Tuhan kami, utuslah di antara mereka seorang rasul dari kalangan mereka sendiri yang akan membacakan kepada mereka ayat-ayat-Mu dan mengajarkan kepada mereka Kitab dan Hikmah serta menyucikan mereka.' 'Kabar gembira Isa' merujuk kepada ayat Al-Quran (61:6) di mana Isa mengumumkan kedatangan Ahmad. Dua bagian Al-Quran yang eksplisit ini — doa Ibrahim dan pengumuman Isa — dipadankan oleh pola nubuatan Perjanjian Lama yang lebih luas yang telah diungkap oleh analisis Deedat.

Sifat ummi Nabi Muhammad ﷺ sendiri adalah data teologis yang signifikan dalam konteks ini. Seorang laki-laki yang tidak dapat membaca atau menulis tidak bisa mencari teks-teks Ibrani dan Yunani dari kitab-kitab suci terdahulu untuk bagian-bagian kenabian yang dapat ia klaim sebagai miliknya sendiri. Pengetahuannya tentang kitab-kitab suci terdahulu — baik informasi akurat yang mereka kandung maupun perubahan-perubahan yang telah mereka alami — datang, sebagaimana tradisi Islam tegaskan, melalui wahyu ilahi. Ini menjelaskan bagaimana Al-Quran dapat secara akurat mengidentifikasi konten kitab-kitab suci terdahulu tanpa Nabi ﷺ memiliki akses ke mereka melalui cara-cara keilmuan yang normal.

Deedat menyimpulkan dengan mencatat bahwa bobot kumulatif dari bukti alkitabiah yang telah ia sajikan — Ulangan 18:18 dengan deskripsinya tentang seorang nabi 'seperti Musa' dari antara saudara-saudara Israel, bagian-bagian Parakletos dalam Injil Yohanes dengan deskripsinya tentang utusan manusia yang akan berbicara apa yang didengarnya dan memuliakan Isa, dan referensi Kidung Agung kepada machmaddim — tidak mudah untuk diabaikan sebagai kebetulan atau interpretasi yang penuh harapan. Setiap potongan bukti memiliki kekuatannya sendiri, dan bersama-sama mereka merupakan kasus yang koheren untuk pengesahan alkitabiah atas kenabian Muhammad ﷺ.

Bagi Muslim yang membaca karya ini, signifikansinya adalah satu konfirmasi dan pendalaman apresiasi: Nabi yang mereka ikuti bukan kecelakaan historis yang tidak terduga melainkan pemenuhan rencana ilahi yang diumumkan sepanjang abad-abad melalui sebuah rantai para nabi. Bagi orang-orang Kristen yang dengan jujur terlibat dengan bukti yang disajikan Deedat, signifikansinya adalah satu undangan: untuk mendekati Nabi Islam bukan sebagai orang asing bagi tradisi mereka sendiri melainkan sebagai seseorang yang dinubuatkan di dalamnya, dan untuk secara serius mempertimbangkan klaim bahwa Al-Quran yang beliau bawa mewakili bentuk akhir dan terpelihara dari firman ilahi yang Allah telah kirimkan kepada seluruh umat manusia.
content_ar: The culminating argument of Deedat's analysis brings together the القرآني declaration of محمد ﷺ's prophethood with the biblical evidence he has surveyed across the preceding الأبواب, demonstrating their mutual coherence and their convergence upon a single conclusion: النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ (ﷺ) wأماetold in the earlier scriptures and his coming represented the fulfillment of the Abrahamic prophetic التراث. The القرآن الكريم explicitly states: 'Those who follow الرسول ﷺ, the unlettered النبي, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel' (7:157) — a الآية that presupposes the existence of prophetic references to محمد ﷺ within the earlier scriptures. This القرآني claim — that the coming of محمد ﷺ was recorded in the Torah and the Gospel — has profound implications for the Islamic الفهم of القرآني reliability. إذا the القرآن الكريم declares, as an empirically verifiable claim, that the earlier scriptures contain prophecies about النبي ﷺ, and إذا those scriptures do indeed contain such passages upon careful examination, this convergence constitutes a powerful argument for the divine origin of the القرآن الكريم. The unlettered man from مكة المكرمة who could not read the Hebrew Torah or the Greek Gospels claimed that they contained prophecies about him — and the textual evidence Deedat has assembled suggests that this claim is well-founded. النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ himself is recorded in the الحديث literature as having said: 'I am the الدعاء of إبراهيم ﷺ, the glad tiding of عيسى, and the dream of my الأم.' The 'الدعاء of إبراهيم ﷺ' refers to إبراهيم ﷺ's الصلاة recorded in the القرآن الكريم (2:129): 'Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your الآيات and teach them the الكتاب and الحكمة and purify them.' The 'glad tiding of عيسى' refers to the القرآني الآية (61:6) in which عيسى ﷺ announces the coming of أحمد. These two explicit القرآني passages — إبراهيم ﷺ's الدعاء and عيسى ﷺ's announcement — are matched by the broader pattern of Old Testament prophecy that Deedat's analysis has uncovered. The unlettered nature of النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ is itself a المهم theological datum in this context. A man who could not read or write could not have searched the Hebrew and Greek texts of the earlier scriptures for prophetic passages to claim as his own. His العلم of the earlier scriptures — both the accurate information they contained and the alterations they had undergone — came, the Islamic التراث affirms, through divine الوحي. This explains how the القرآن الكريم can accurately identify the content of the earlier scriptures without النبي ﷺ having access to them through normal العلمي means.
